# The gherkin HTML page is generated by a shipped script, not a runtime viewer

Gherkin to HTML is implemented as `scripts/gherkin_to_html.py` (stdlib-only
Python, shipped with the skill): it scans the project, parses the Gherkin, and
writes one self-contained HTML file as the project's reading entry. The page is
a faithful, as-is rendering of the project's `.feature` files at generation time
— easier to read and search, never summarized (which is why the capability is
named "Gherkin to HTML", not an "overview").

The scan covers all project-owned Gherkin files, not only files under
`features/`. This lets projects keep specifications in `docs/specs/`, examples,
or domain directories while still getting one reading entry. Hidden and
dependency directories are excluded.

The generated file presents that snapshot as a Gherkin Reader: Gherkin file tabs,
scenario counts, collapsible scenarios, phase spacing between Given/When/Then,
search/filtering, local themes, and Chinese/English UI labels plus displayed
Gherkin keywords. The renderer currently supports English Gherkin and
Simplified Chinese Gherkin files that declare `# language: zh-CN`; other
localized Gherkin languages are outside the current support scope. These are
rendering affordances only. The display language can localize Gherkin keywords
without rewriting the source text, and it does not change the source-of-truth
status of the `.feature` files.

The script keeps a lightweight reader parser in the standard library instead of
depending on the official Gherkin parser. This keeps the shipped skill runnable
after a plain file copy. The trade-off is that the reader parser is not the
authority for Gherkin correctness; it supports the common structures the reader
needs and surfaces parse warnings for anything it cannot understand.

We considered shipping only a self-contained HTML *viewer* instead — no Python,
the page itself reads the project's Gherkin files in browser JavaScript.
Rejected: the browser sandbox forbids a `file://` page from reading the disk on
its own, so a viewer can only work through a folder-pick or drag-and-drop
authorization on open (with persistence limited to Chromium). That trades the
double-click-and-read experience for a manual step every visit, and it moves the
parsing/rendering logic into browser JS, where verifying the BDD scenarios would
require browser-automation test infrastructure instead of the existing
pytest-bdd subprocess steps.

## Consequences

- Generating the page requires Python 3 on the machine — already a given, since
  the skill's sync hook runs `check_bdd_sync.py` the same way.
- The page is a project-level reading entry and a snapshot: after editing
  Gherkin files, regenerate it. The generated file is an artifact, not source.
  By default it is written to `docs/gherkin.html`, creating `docs/` when needed;
  this repo gitignores generated `gherkin.html` files. Other projects decide
  for themselves whether to track the generated page; the skill does not force
  their `.gitignore` policy.
- The scenarios in `features/gherkin-to-html.feature` stay testable by driving
  the script as a subprocess and asserting on the emitted HTML; no browser in
  the test loop.
- Parse warnings are reader tolerance, not validation success. The page should
  show broken Gherkin files rather than hide them; syntax and execution
  correctness remain the job of the project's tests.
- The reader parser is deliberately smaller than the official Gherkin grammar.
  Expanding language or syntax support is acceptable when it improves reading,
  but conformance belongs to pytest-bdd, Cucumber, or the installed project's chosen
  Gherkin runner.
- An agent can render the page and hand it over (e.g. attach it to a session)
  without any user interaction, which a pick-a-folder viewer could not do
  headlessly.
